metadata.label: Title
metadata.value: ("Voyage of discovery to the North Pacific Ocean and round the world; in which the coast of north-west America has been carefully examined and accurately surveyed ... Atlas.")

metadata.label: Description
metadata.value: Atlas only , without title, as issued. Contains ten charts and six sheets of views (profiles of parts of the coastlines). Compare the English charts to the French issues in the French Neptune of 1820. Vancouver's charts were the most accurate of the area for many years into the 19th century. In addition to the eight Northwest Coast charts, there is a "Chart shewing part of the S.W. Coast of New Holland" (with inset charts of New Zealand) and "A Chart of the Sandwich Islands." Maps are without color and bound into half leather burgandy cloth covered boards with "Atlas To Vancouvers Voyage" stamped in gilt on a red leather label on the spine.

metadata.label: Title
metadata.value: The Harbour of San Francisco, Nueva California. The Entrance to San Francisco Harbour. By Captn. F.W. Beechy ... 1827 & 8

metadata.label: Description
metadata.value: Engraved charts and several views on one sheet. Chart No. 591. From the Warren Heckrotte Auction catalog: "Very rare and important chart of San Francisco Bay, the result of the first scientific mapping of the Bay. Neil Harlow states that the chart "had a wide influence upon later maps of the area. The chart, with copies and adaptations of it, served to the end of the Mexican period and formed the substantial basis of the earliest ones produced under the American regime. It was deficient only in the region beyond Carquinez Strait..." Harlow notes the chart of the entrance contains "additional hydrographic data pertinent to entering the port and reaching the chief places of anchorage. Accompanying the chart are elevation views depicting the approaches to the bay and the hazards to navigation." No copies are listed in OCLC/WorldCat.."

metadata.label: Description
metadata.value: References: Harlow, Maps of S.F. Bay, 20 & 21, ( Rep ). TMC, 12/83 & 3/84, ( Rep ) - two articles on Admiralty charts. Orozco y Berra, 1433.

metadata.label: Title
metadata.value: Chart of the North Pacific Ocean, Exhibiting the Eastern Shores of Asia and the Western Coast of North America from the Equator to Behring's Strait with Plans, on an Enlarged Scale, of the Principal Islands and Harbours. Compiled from the most Recent Surveys made by order of the British, French, and United States Governments. London. Published by James Imray. Chart and Nautical Bookseller, 102 Minores, 1849. (insets) Sandwich Islands (Hawaii); Philippine Islands; Harbor of San Francisco; Victoria Harbor; Mouth of the Columbia River; Marian or Ladrone Islands; Awatska Harbor in Kamchatka

metadata.label: Description
metadata.value: Shows area 0-69 degrees North. The chart shows much detail around San Francisco Bay at the start of the California Gold Rush. This chart was evidently aboard the ship, Northern Light. Handwritten on the chart are the ship's locations on a round trip from San Francisco - Acapulco (March-April,1852) and thence toward Boston in May 1852. According to Wikipedia: The Northern Light was an American clipper ship. In 1853 she sailed from San Francisco, California to Boston, Massachusetts via Cape Horn with Captain Freeman Hatch at the helm in a record-setting 76 days, 6 hours. The record still stands for a single hull vessel. Launched September 25, 1851, the ship was abandoned at sea, January 2, 1862 after colliding with the French brig Nouveau St. Jacques. on a transatlantic voyage. A blueback chartp. Relief shown by hachures. Mercator projection. An unusual canvas tube that held the rolled chart.

metadata.label: Relation
metadata.value: David Rumsey Map Collection at Stanford University Libraries

metadata.label: Pub Note
metadata.value: 1st edition, Cowen gives precedence. Issued before the larger edition of 1851 (see our 4658.000) , this is in the form of a pocket map with only the uncolored general chart of the bay, but with the 48 pages of text printed on pages small enough to fit into the pocket map covers. No views, as issued.

metadata.label: Title
metadata.value: Maps and Charts of the United States Coast Survey. A.D. Bache, Superintendent. To July, 1854

metadata.label: Description
metadata.value: Not an Annual Report! Unique edition-all engraved. A set of most of the Coast Survey maps published to July 1854, and all are engraved instead of lithographed as were the maps in the annual reports. Each map has the unique inscription at the top reading "Presented under Authority of an Act of Congress of the United States of June 3d 1844 by direction of the Treasury Department A.D. Bache Superintendent Coast Survey." U.S. Atlases lists another edition (L3666) of 1856. Laid in is a broadside published by the Survey: "Coast Survey Charts Published, Sketches and Preliminary Charts, The Following Sketches will be Gratuitously Distributed:, Agents for Sale of Maps:, Coast Survey Office, Washington, March 15, 1853" (our no. 5754). Bound with half leather and marble paper covered boards with title in gilt on spine.

metadata.label: Relation
metadata.value: David Rumsey Map Collection at Stanford University Libraries

items.label: Modified sextant with spirit-level horizon.. Sextant with Spirit-Level Horizon, with Associated Dipleidoscope

canvases.label: Modified sextant with spirit-level horizon.. Sextant with Spirit-Level Horizon, with Associated Dipleidoscope

metadata.label: Short Title
metadata.value: Modified sextant with spirit-level horizon.. Sextant with Spirit-Level Horizon, with Associated Dipleidoscope

metadata.label: Note
metadata.value: Close-up of sextant instrument itself. Modified sextant with spirit-level horizon. A standard brass and steel sextant, modified by George Davidson to include a spirit-level horizon for inland observations where no sea horizon was available. Likely made by Troughton & Simms, London, c. 1865.

metadata.label: Full Title
metadata.value: Modified sextant with spirit-level horizon.. Sextant with Spirit-Level Horizon, with Associated Dipleidoscope

metadata.label: Pub Title
metadata.value: Sextant with Spirit-Level Horizon, with Associated Dipleidoscope

metadata.label: Pub Note
metadata.value: Sextant with Spirit-Level Horizon, with Associated Dipleidoscope Owner/Exhibitor: George Davidson (1825–1911), U.S. Coast Survey Maker: Sextant likely Troughton & Simms, London (frame style, c. 1860s) Modification: Spirit-level horizon attachment devised by Davidson (Journal of the Franklin Institute, 1866) Associated Instrument: Dipleidoscope by Elliott Bros., London, in fitted leather case Provenance: Exhibited at the Exposition Universelle, Paris, 1867 (Group 2, Class 12, No. 63) Description Sextant: Precision arc instrument, with telescope and mirrors, modified by Davidson to carry a spirit-level horizon for use where no sea horizon was visible. Engraved nameplate: “George Davidson, Philadelphia.” Original wooden case with painted inscription recording its entry in the Paris Exposition of 1867. Dipleidoscope: Compact brass instrument with multiple glass prisms and attached telescope, inscribed “Elliott Bros., 449 West Strand, London.” Contained in original velvet-lined morocco leather case. Historical Context The Exposition Universelle in Paris (April–November 1867) drew international exhibits of scientific and industrial progress. Group 2, Class 12 covered “instruments of precision.” Davidson entered his modified sextant on behalf of the U.S. Coast Survey; official reports list “Davidson, G., Washington; sextant.” Jury accounts record that the instrument earned honorable mention. The sextant illustrates U.S. Coast Survey field adaptations of standard British instruments for rugged inland and coastal survey work. The accompanying dipleidoscope reflects Davidson’s broader toolkit: a portable device for determining true noon by merging solar images, allowing accurate chronometer checks without a full observatory transit. Subsequent History Case markings show later shipment: “From T. D. Davidson, 1721 Washington St., S.F., Cal. — To Hon. F. D. Roosevelt, Naval Observatory, Washington D.C.” Roosevelt, Assistant Secretary of the Navy (1913–1920), oversaw Naval Observatory instruments. The valuation was listed as $100. Significance Demonstrates Davidson’s role as both surveyor and instrument innovator. Connects the instrument to the Paris Exposition of 1867, a major stage for scientific and industrial display. Association with Franklin D. Roosevelt in his Navy Department role adds a later layer of provenance. The inclusion of the dipleidoscope reveals Davidson’s combined use of angular and temporal instruments to replicate observatory precision in the field. Exhibition History Paris Exposition, 1867, Group 2, Class 12, No. 63 (scientific instruments). Provenance Timeline c. 1865 — Sextant manufactured; Davidson modifies with spirit-level horizon. 1866 — Davidson publishes description in Journal of the Franklin Institute. 1867 — Exhibited in Paris, Exposition Universelle. 1867–1880s — Active Coast Survey use in western U.S. and Alaska. Early 20th century — Shipped by Davidson to Franklin D. Roosevelt at the Naval Observatory, Washington D.C. Later — Preserved with original sextant case and associated dipleidoscope. Bibliography Davidson, George. “Sextant with Attached Spirit-Level Horizon.” Journal of the Franklin Institute, 1866. Paris Universal Exposition, 1867. Reports of the United States Commissioners (Washington, 1869). Catalogue général, Exposition universelle de 1867, Paris, Group 2, Class 12. Jury Reports, Group II, Instruments of Precision (noting honorable mention).

items.label: City Of San Francisco And Its Vicinity, California.

canvases.label: City Of San Francisco And Its Vicinity, California.

metadata.label: Short Title
metadata.value: City Of San Francisco And Its Vicinity, California.

metadata.label: Note
metadata.value: Heavy paper chart; uses contours. First issued in 1857, with another edition issued later in 1857 that differs from the earlier 1857 edition and is apparently the same as this 1859 edition (Vogdes). Without color. Map is bordered in blue silk, dissected into 8 sections, and folds into a new mauve cloth folding case with "U.S. Coast Survey San Francisco 1859" stamped in gilt on the spine.

metadata.label: Full Title
metadata.value: U.S. Coast Survey A.D. Bache, Superintendent. City Of San Francisco And Its Vicinity California. Topography by A.F. Rodgers, Sub-assistant. Hydrography by the Party under the command of Lieut. R.M. Cuyler N.S.N. Assistant. Published in 1859. U.S. Coast Survey Office. Price 40 cents. Verified W.R. Palmer Capt. Topl. Engrs. Asst. C.S. In charge of Office. Lith. of J. Bien 60 Fulton St. N.Y.

metadata.label: Title
metadata.value: N. America West Coast. San Francisco Harbour Surveyed by Lieut. James Alden U.S. Navy 1856. London. Published at the Admiralty 20th. Oct. 1860, under the Superintendence of Captn. Washington, R.N., FR.S. Hydrographer. Corrections to Augt. 63 ... Engraved by J. & C. Walker. (with 4 profile views)

metadata.label: Description
metadata.value: Large format British Admiralty chart of West Coast of the United States. Showing San Francisco Bay, extending to Drake Bay, Vallejo, Petaluma Creek, and Oakland, as surveyed by James Alden of the U.S. Coast Survey and the British shortly after the discovery of Gold in California. Shows inland detail identifying roads, landings, buildings, and detailed street grid in major cities. Includes topographic features. Relief shown by hachures and spot heights in feet. Depth shown by soundings in Fathoms. Includes text, notes, and 3 rhumb lines pointing to the north.

metadata.label: Description
metadata.value: With manuscript notes dated June 19, 1906, by George Davidson, regarding the earthquake of January 9, 1857 which caused the release of huge amounts of subterranean gas in the bay, killing large numbers of fish. Chart is without color.

metadata.label: Relation
metadata.value: David Rumsey Map Collection at Stanford University Libraries

metadata.label: Note
metadata.value: Nautical chart of San Francisco Bay entrance, Printed without color. Relief shown by contours and spot heights. Depths shown by soundings. Includes text, tide chart, and chart of lighthouses and signal stations. "No. 621a., Electrotype copy no. 1 by U.S.C.& G.S." Plate No. 1829.

metadata.label: Pub Title
metadata.value: Log Of H.M.S. "Swiftsure" Bearing the (flag of) Rear Admiral H.M.L. Lyons commanded by Captain C. Mainwaring. C.F. Henderson.

metadata.label: Pub Reference
metadata.value: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HMS_Swiftsure_(1870) is a short illustrated article in Wikipedia on the Swiftsure.

metadata.label: Pub Note
metadata.value: This is a manuscript ship's log that begins to record a journey midway from Honolulu to Esquimalt in British Columbia, then down the west coast of the United States and the west coast of South America, around Cape Horn, and back to England. It begins in July, 1884 and ends in November, 1885. 16 beautifully drawn charts record the track of the steam and sail powered ship, showing stops in San Francisco, Mazatlan, San Blas, Valparaiso and Rio de Janeiro and other ports. Many drawings illustrate aspects of the ship and its equipment. The log itself records the weather and daily ship operations. An unusual highly illustrated document. Swiftsure is the name assigned to ten successive ships of the Royal (British) Navy, the first launched in 1573 and the most recent in 1971. This one was commissioned in 1871 and was the lead ship of the Swiftsure class battleships built in the late Victorian era. It operated under steam and sail and was ironclad. It was very fast for its time, 13.75 knots. It served as the Pacific Station flagship from 1882-1885 and 1888-1890. Renamed "Orontes" in 1901, she was scrapped in 1908.
